Visually, the redesign is...whatever. Better than the redesigned iCal on my Mac, at least. But what bugs me about this update is that the locations are inaccurate and vary, even when the iPhone you're hunting hasn't moved. I just tried it with my boyfriend's phone (which is sitting in plain view on his nightstand), and after refreshing the map about 30 times in total, his phone was shown as being anywhere from in the apartment to three and four blocks away in any direction. A 16-square-block radius? That's not very helpful when I've lost my phone in downtown Boston. If this was a limitation of Apple's GPS technology, then fine. But before the update, this app was always pinpoint accurate. (Believe me, I've monitored my boyfriend's walk home from campus with the previous version of the app...and yes, I had his permission to do so. It was extremely accurate). The other thing that bugs me is the location and size of the "wipe" button, whig is now on the map page. It's on the bottom right corner, aka the location that I always accidentally bump when I hold my phone with one hand. I don't know if there's a confirmation after pressing the wipe button (according to another reviewer, the Lock feature doesn't), and I'm too afraid to find out. The bf might me okay with me occasionally spying on his journeys around the city with this app, but I don't think he'd appreciate it if I wiped his phone in the name of curiosity.

I posted a moderate review and it's gone now? What's the use in having a reviews section if reviews are going to be gamed by the developers and/or Apple? The idea of Bumble is great. The execution of the idea is a abysmal. Bumble gives you 300 characters to make a profile. That's two tweets and is ludicrous. What can you possibly do with this that is actually useful? Bumble doesn't force users to type anything into their profile either, so almost every profile I see (the few Bumble bothers to show me) are completely blank. There's nothing to do but swipe right for "attractive" or left for "[all other possible judgments]". There's no depth or subtlety. On OKC you can choose to be shallow or impulsive. On Bumble, it's enforced. Bumble shows you people you've just swiped left on. Repeatedly. EDIT: It does this for people you've swiped right on, too! Hint: there are few actual profiles! Bumble shows you the same five people repeatedly until you swipe on them (and even then, will show them again later). There's no way to skip a profile, unless you change your range setting to force it to reshuffle, which doesn't work half the time either (I understand this isn't the intended usage; it's me trying to get more from this crippled "tool"). If a user's profile is empty, you still have to pass judgment on them, without any information to form meaningful judgments. Utterly inhuman. Bumble has no way to filter for basic preferences like smoking, religion, relationship style, etc., nor any way to list interests. Bumble repeatedly asks for a rating and feedback, but it's clear that this feedback does not result in improvements. The responses to legit complaints here in the reviews is clear evidence enough of that. The "verify" feature is not presented to the user at all. There's no place to see that you've done it. I found this nearly invisible feature by accident when the icon appeared on one profile out of the thirty I've seen. The first time I tried to use the verify feature, both buttons on the second screen failed to do anything at all and I had to close the app via iOS task switching. It's also apparently an automated feature using image matching algorithms, which is pretty pointless because software cannot recognize human faces all that well. Spend time with google's reverse image search and you'll see how unreliable machine "vision" is. Also, the gesture requested was taken by a professional photographer, not a selfie taken by the model posing in the image. There's no way to match the composition and body position to what he's doing in that photo while you're also holding the camera at arm's length and struggling to take a photo with an on-screen button. The fact that the verification claims a success tells me that the image matching is so severely compromised (to make it function with the specified images) as to be utterly useless. It's a false sense of security and nothing more. Bumble's usage of school & employment as descriptors is utterly useless. It further puts the focus on surface features, only in this case the surface feature isn't as remotely useful as a photo: did the person lie about the job or school? What does the job title mean? Are they using their schooling? Why does this even matter? I know why: this reflects what the developers think defines people's value. Most troubling of all is Bumble's requirement to use your real first name as found on Facebook. This may get your Facebook profile stalked, if your name is somewhat unique (or if the stalker is lucky enough in their Facebook searches). This is something I mentioned in my now missing prior review. No comments from the developer, just a deleted review. That's not legit usage of a review system. It's abuse by the developer. This is a safety risk to people and should be addressed, not suppressed in reviews!!! Bumble's photo management is awful. Cropping is nonexistent & the system blows up the images beyond their correct size unless you view them in "full screen" mode. This makes poor photos look worse and makes people more uncomfortable about the photos they're already uncomfortable about. As a photographer, I'm somewhat inoculated against this when seeing myself, but Bumble's developers are doing nothing at all to accommodate the majority of people who hate seeing photos of themselves. Bumble does not remove inactive profiles from rotation. Find people on Facebook and you can confirm they're involved with someone (and likely monogamous, but Bumble has no place to specify relationship type; either they're cheating or they're inactive users). Overall, this app seems to have been made by Silicon Valley elitist entrepreneurs who aren't dating, don't need dating apps, and who think that the entirety of human diversity boils down to the kinds of attributes most important to the privileged and homogeneous population of elite entrepreneurs: • your photo • your school name • your job title Hot tip: average human beings don't consider this enough data to judge whether they like or dislike someone, nor whether they want to risk communicating (especially the population that has been left with online dating as their only option). If people preferred these three attributes only, they might as well just go to bars and clubs... the things that online dating tool users aren't doing, for various reasons! I'm not deluded into thinking that these apps don't make money. Obviously they do, and obviously that's all that matters to the companies developing them. These apps have nothing at all to do with helping people. If they DO help people, it's in spite of the app's design and intent, not because of any goal by the developer. Dating apps are a particularly grotesque type of exploitation of humanity. They make the matching process more frustrating just to lure users into paying for upgrades ("Boost" in Bumble terms).

The free version will only let you connect via WiFi. It's pretty much only good for using as a baby monitor or whomever you may want to "spy" on. You can only use this version on your home network. The paid version allows you to connect via 3G and WiFi. 3G is slow but still works. The rest of the world has 4G why is the US so slow to install this upgrade? Sure the video is a bit choppy; playing back at around 1 or 2 frames per second. But hey, the damn thing works over the 3G network! It's plenty clear for me to see what's going on at home. Audio is a bit laggy too but you can adjust that in the app. I just haven't bothered. Also, if you're phone is connecting through the same WiFi your computer is hooked up to, the app will crash. And there's no getting around that. So take your phone over to your buddies place, jump on his WiFi and fire up the AirCam app there with no problems. You just can't use the same WiFi as your computer. Now configuring my ATT U-Verse router firewall was a real pain. If the auto port-forwarding feature doesn't work with your router and you know absolutely nothing about IP addresses, Port configuration and firewalls, prepare to be frustrated and have plenty of time on your hands to manually configure it. The support provided by senstic is a good reference but there are just so many different routers out there with so many variations of each that their support only provides a good basic detail of how to manually configure. But if you can see past the obvious differences in the photos they provide, you should be able to configure it with no problem. Otherwise call over one of your more "computer savvy" buds
